message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Since COVID-19 erupted in the United States, little is known about how state-level opioid overdose trends and decedent characteristics have varied throughout the country.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Investigate changes in annual overdose death rates, substances involved, and decedent demographics in opioid overdose deaths across nine states; assess whether 2019-2020 trends were emerging (i.e., change from 2019-2020 was non-existent from 2018-2019) or continuing (i.e., change from 2019-2020 existed from 2018-2019).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cross-sectional study using vital statistics data to conduct a retrospective analysis comparing 2020 to 2019 and 2019 to 2018 across nine states.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Alaska, Colorado, Connecticut, Indiana, Massachusetts, North Carolina, Rhode Island, Utah, and Wyoming.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Opioid-related overdose deaths in 2018, 2019, and 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Measurements</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Annual overdose death rate, proportion of overdose deaths involving specific substances, and decedent demographics (age, sex, race, and ethnicity).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We find emerging increases in annual opioid-related overdose death rates in Alaska (55.3% [
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.020]), Colorado (80.2% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), Indiana (40.1% [
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.038]), North Carolina (30.5% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), and Rhode Island (29.6% [
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.011]). Decreased heroin-involved overdose deaths were emerging in Alaska (−49.5% [
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.001]) and Indiana (−58.8% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), and continuing in Colorado (−33.3% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), Connecticut (−48.2% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), Massachusetts (39.9% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), and North Carolina (−34.8% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]). Increases in synthetic opioid presence were emerging in Alaska (136.5% [
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.019]) and Indiana (27.6% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), and continuing in Colorado (44.4% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]), Connecticut (3.6% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.05]), and North Carolina (14.6% [
                    <jats:italic>P<</jats:italic>
                    0.001]). We find emerging increases in the proportion of male decedents in Colorado (15.2% [
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.008]) and Indiana (12.0% [
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.013]).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Limitations</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Delays from state-specific death certification processes resulted in varying analysis periods across states.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>These findings highlight emerging changes in opioid overdose dynamics across different states, which can inform state-specific public health interventions.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Emerging and Continuing Trends in Opioid Overdose Decedent Characteristics during COVID-19

reference.article-title: When Epidemics Collide: Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) and the Opioid Crisis
